{
  "topic": "Advanced System Design Mastery",
  "topic_slug": "advanced-system-design-mastery",
  "level": "Expert / First-Principles",
  "goal": "Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.",

      "id": 1,
      "title": "Hardware and OS Primitives (The Physics of Computing)",
      "estimated_minutes": 150,
      "description": "Before designing distributed systems, you must master the absolute physical constraints of a single machine. High-level abstractions fail when they ignore hardware realities. This module establishes why systems bottleneck by examining CPU concurrency, memory hierarchies, and storage mechanics at the bare-metal level.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Quantify single-machine constraints, calculate system bottlenecks, and evaluate I/O and concurrency models at the OS kernel level.",

          "Latencies Every Programmer Should Know",
          "L1/L2/L3 Cache vs. Main Memory vs. Disk overhead",
          "Kernel vs. User space transitions",
          "Context Switching mechanics and overhead",

          "Linux epoll vs. io_uring",
          "SSD Storage Internals: Page vs. Block",
          "Sequential vs. Random write performance and amplification"
        ],
        "sourceRefs": [

        "goal": "Deconstruct transport layer protocols to analyze connection management, flow control, and latency optimization techniques.",

      "plan": {
        "goal": "Design efficient inter-service communication architectures by selecting optimal application-layer protocols and serialization formats.",

      "estimated_minutes": 150,
      "description": "A database is not a black box; it is an abstraction over hardware I/O. To scale data storage, you must understand how a single machine writes to disk. This module contrasts read-optimized and write-optimized data structures at the binary level.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Analyze database storage structures and indexing mechanics to optimize read and write paths for specific workload profiles.",

      "estimated_minutes": 150,
      "description": "When multiple users access data simultaneously, state corruption is inevitable without rigorous concurrency control. This module provides a theoretical and practical deep-dive into transaction isolation, moving far beyond a basic definition of ACID.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Master ACID semantics and evaluate the strict mathematical trade-offs between database isolation levels and concurrent access phenomena.",

      "estimated_minutes": 180,
      "description": "Moving from one machine to a cluster introduces the unpredictable chaos of network partitions and clock drift. This module covers the core mathematical theorems and consensus algorithms that make reliable distributed systems possible.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Navigate the fundamental impossibilities of distributed computing, analyze logical time, and trace state agreement via consensus algorithms.",

          "PACELC Theorem extensions for latency",

      "title": "Distributed Data: Partitioning and Replication",
      "estimated_minutes": 150,
      "description": "To store petabytes of data, databases must span hundreds of machines. This module explores how to partition data without creating massive hotspots, and how to replicate it while managing the delicate balance of consistency and availability.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Design horizontally scalable data stores using advanced partitioning formulas, consistent hashing, and quorum-based replication.",
        "concepts": [

          "Shard Key selection criteria and the Hot Key problem",

      "id": 8,

      "estimated_minutes": 150,
      "description": "Serving millions of concurrent users requires aggressively offloading work from primary databases and intelligently distributing network traffic. This module covers load balancing, caching hierarchies, and asynchronous architectures.",
      "plan": {
        "goal": "Architect traffic distribution, caching tiers, and asynchronous event-driven workflows to handle massive user scale without degradation.",
        "concepts": [

parsed.goal: Professional mastery through first-principles engineering; building a bottom-up mental model of large-scale systems.

modules.title: Hardware and OS Primitives (The Physics of Computing)
modules.description: Before designing distributed systems, you must master the absolute physical constraints of a single machine. High-level abstractions fail when they ignore hardware realities. This module establishes why systems bottleneck by examining CPU concurrency, memory hierarchies, and storage mechanics at the bare-metal level.

plan.goal: Quantify single-machine constraints, calculate system bottlenecks, and evaluate I/O and concurrency models at the OS kernel level.
concepts: Latencies Every Programmer Should Know
L1/L2/L3 Cache vs. Main Memory vs. Disk overhead
Kernel vs. User space transitions
Context Switching mechanics and overhead
Process vs. Thread vs. Coroutine
User-space threads (Goroutines/Green threads)
Blocking vs. Non-blocking I/O
Synchronous vs. Asynchronous I/O
Linux epoll vs. io_uring
SSD Storage Internals: Page vs. Block
Sequential vs. Random write performance and amplification

modules.title: Network Internals and the Transport Layer
modules.description: Distributed systems are only as resilient as the wires connecting them. Surface-level knowledge stops at HTTP. This module dives into the transport layer to show exactly how packets traverse networks, why latency spikes occur, and how modern protocols bypass ancient bottlenecks.

plan.goal: Deconstruct transport layer protocols to analyze connection management, flow control, and latency optimization techniques.
concepts: OSI Model transport layer specifics
TCP 3-way handshake mechanics and latency cost
TCP Flow Control and Sliding Windows
TCP Congestion Control: Slow Start and Congestion Avoidance
UDP datagram characteristics and use cases
Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking in TCP
HTTP/1.1 vs. HTTP/2 multiplexing constraints
QUIC protocol foundations
HTTP/3 over UDP mechanics

modules.title: Application Protocols and Data Serialization
modules.description: Once a connection is established, the format of the data being transmitted dictates CPU and network overhead. This module covers the architectural trade-offs of API paradigms and why binary serialization is critical for evolving large-scale systems.

plan.goal: Design efficient inter-service communication architectures by selecting optimal application-layer protocols and serialization formats.
concepts: REST constraints: Statelessness and Idempotency
Remote Procedure Calls (RPC) architecture
gRPC multiplexing and streaming capabilities
WebSockets for bidirectional stateful communication
Parsing overhead: JSON and XML vs. Binary formats
Binary Serialization mechanics
Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) internals and memory layout
Avro and Thrift structural comparisons
Schema evolution mechanics in distributed services
prerequisites: TCP Flow Control and Sliding Windows

modules.title: Single-Node Storage Engines and Indexing
modules.description: A database is not a black box; it is an abstraction over hardware I/O. To scale data storage, you must understand how a single machine writes to disk. This module contrasts read-optimized and write-optimized data structures at the binary level.

plan.goal: Analyze database storage structures and indexing mechanics to optimize read and write paths for specific workload profiles.
concepts: Storage engine read vs. write optimization trade-offs
B-Tree and B+Tree data structure mechanics
Log-Structured Merge-Trees (LSM-Trees) architecture
MemTable and Write-Ahead Log (WAL) synergy
SSTable immutability and Compaction strategies
Clustered vs. Non-clustered index layouts
Composite index binary structure and left-prefix matching
Covered queries and index-only scans
The computational overhead of index maintenance
prerequisites: SSD Storage Internals: Page vs. Block
Sequential vs. Random write performance and amplification

modules.title: Transaction Theory and Concurrency Control
modules.description: When multiple users access data simultaneously, state corruption is inevitable without rigorous concurrency control. This module provides a theoretical and practical deep-dive into transaction isolation, moving far beyond a basic definition of ACID.

plan.goal: Master ACID semantics and evaluate the strict mathematical trade-offs between database isolation levels and concurrent access phenomena.
concepts: ACID properties formal definition and nuances
ANSI SQL Transaction Isolation Levels
Read Uncommitted vs. Read Committed
Repeatable Read vs. Serializable execution
Concurrency phenomena: Dirty reads, Non-repeatable reads, Phantom reads
Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC) implementation
Pessimistic vs. Optimistic locking mechanisms
Deadlock detection and resolution
prerequisites: B-Tree and B+Tree data structure mechanics

modules.title: Distributed Systems Theory: Time, State, and Consensus
modules.description: Moving from one machine to a cluster introduces the unpredictable chaos of network partitions and clock drift. This module covers the core mathematical theorems and consensus algorithms that make reliable distributed systems possible.

plan.goal: Navigate the fundamental impossibilities of distributed computing, analyze logical time, and trace state agreement via consensus algorithms.
concepts: The 8 Fallacies of Distributed Computing
CAP Theorem mathematical trade-offs
PACELC Theorem extensions for latency
Clock Synchronization challenges and NTP limits
Wall Clock Time vs. Logical Time
Lamport Clocks and causality
Vector Clocks and concurrent event detection
The necessity of Consensus Algorithms
Paxos theoretical complexity vs. Raft understandability
Raft mechanics: Leader Election and Log Replication
sourceRefs: Raft: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm
prerequisites: TCP Flow Control and Sliding Windows

modules.title: Distributed Data: Partitioning and Replication
modules.description: To store petabytes of data, databases must span hundreds of machines. This module explores how to partition data without creating massive hotspots, and how to replicate it while managing the delicate balance of consistency and availability.

plan.goal: Design horizontally scalable data stores using advanced partitioning formulas, consistent hashing, and quorum-based replication.
concepts: Horizontal partitioning (Sharding) vs. vertical scaling
Shard Key selection criteria and the Hot Key problem
Consistent Hashing mathematics
Hash rings and Virtual Nodes for load distribution
Quorums formula (R + W > N)
Tuning Read/Write quorums for speed vs. integrity
Gossip Protocols for decentralized state propagation
Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store principles
Bigtable: Distributed Storage System for Structured Data principles
sourceRefs: Dynamo: Amazon’s Highly Available Key-value Store
Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data
prerequisites: Raft mechanics: Leader Election and Log Replication

modules.title: Scalability Patterns and Traffic Management
modules.description: Serving millions of concurrent users requires aggressively offloading work from primary databases and intelligently distributing network traffic. This module covers load balancing, caching hierarchies, and asynchronous architectures.

plan.goal: Architect traffic distribution, caching tiers, and asynchronous event-driven workflows to handle massive user scale without degradation.
concepts: Layer 4 (Transport) vs. Layer 7 (Application) Load Balancing
Load Balancing Algorithms: Least Connections, IP Hash
Caching Strategies: Cache Aside, Write-Through, Write-Behind
Cache Eviction Policies: LRU, LFU, ARC
The Cache Stampede / Thundering Herd problem and mitigations
Asynchronous Processing architectural patterns
Message Brokers mechanics (Kafka append-only logs vs. RabbitMQ queues)
Message Delivery guarantees: At-least-once, At-most-once, Exactly-once

modules.title: Resilience, Observability, and Advanced Architecture
modules.description: Systems will fail. A mature architecture expects failure and is designed to heal itself automatically. This module shifts focus from scaling up to surviving catastrophe, ensuring systems degrade gracefully and remain observable.

plan.goal: Design self-healing, observable distributed systems implementing advanced fault-tolerance patterns and isolated execution cells.
concepts: Distributed Failure Modes and cascading failures
Circuit Breaker pattern states and thresholds
Retries with Exponential Backoff and Jitter
Rate Limiting algorithms: Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window
The Three Pillars of Observability: Metrics, Logging, Tracing
Event Sourcing principles and auditability
Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS)
Microservices network overhead and distributed monolith risks
Cell-Based Architecture for blast radius isolation

modules.title: Capstone: Deconstructing Global-Scale Systems
modules.description: Theoretical knowledge must be mapped to reality. This capstone applies every principle learned—from L1 caches to B-Trees to Consensus algorithms—by architecting a global video streaming platform akin to Netflix.

plan.goal: Synthesize hardware, network, storage, and distributed systems primitives to architect and mathematically justify a global-scale streaming service.
concepts: System Bottleneck Identification (CPU vs. RAM vs. Disk vs. Network)
Distributed Worker Queues for mass data ingestion and transcoding
Object Storage (S3) vs. Block Storage for media
Distributed NoSQL deployment for metadata high availability
Edge computing and Content Delivery Networks (CDN) caching
Anycast DNS routing and geographical latency optimization
Graceful failover and circuit breaking in complex topologies
The Google File System (GFS) paper principles applied
MapReduce paradigm for distributed data processing
sourceRefs: Worked Example: Designing a Global Video Streaming Service
MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
prerequisites: Circuit Breaker pattern states and thresholds
Message Brokers mechanics (Kafka append-only logs vs. RabbitMQ queues)
Edge computing and Content Delivery Networks (CDN) caching

modules.title: The Physics of a Single Machine: Hardware & OS Primitives
Network Internals and Transport Layer Mechanics
Storage Engine Mechanics and Indexing Depth
Distributed Foundations: Time, Consensus, and Consistency
Hardware and OS Primitives (The Physics of Computing)
